chunk_summary: Adolescents (10-18 years) require macronutrient-focused nutrition strategies addressing growth, energy needs, and risks like sugary beverages, erratic meals, and body-image pressures. Emphasis is on balanced meals, avoiding pitfalls like protein powders and energy drinks, and addressing red flags like eating disorders or obesity-related complications.
learning_objectives: Understand macronutrient priorities for adolescents aged 10-18 years, including protein and energy needs.
Identify and address dietary pitfalls such as sugary beverages, skipping meals, and unregulated supplements.
Recognize red flags like rapid weight loss, binge-purge behavior, or obesity-related complications requiring escalation.
Promote family-based dietary interventions and scheduled follow-ups for sustainable outcomes.
key_facts: Adolescents experience rapid linear growth and higher energy expenditure, requiring tailored macronutrient strategies.
BMI-for-age charts and diet-beverage-activity screens are essential for assessing nutritional priorities.
Sugary beverages and energy drinks bypass fullness signals and contribute to weight gain.
Sports drinks are only recommended for prolonged high-intensity activity, not routine school days.
Red flags include rapid weight loss, binge-purge behavior, amenorrhea, syncope, depression, or suspected eating disorders.
Obesity-related complications like hypertension, NAFLD (raised ALT), or T2DM symptoms require escalation.
reference_quote: Liquid calories bypass fullness signals. First we remove sugar-sweetened beverages and energy drinks, then adjust snack quality; we do not crash diet or cut protein.
